title: 13 Planning Phases
content: The Marigold organizes wedding planning into 13 sequenced phases — from engagement and budget to post-wedding admin. Each phase has its own checklist, vendor briefs, and moodboards so the bride is never working out of order. The 13 phases cover sangeet, mehndi, haldi, ceremony, and reception as distinct planning tracks.

title: 582 Planning Tasks
content: The full Marigold checklist is 582 tasks across all 13 phases. Tasks are pre-tagged by ceremony, vendor, and timeline. The checklist is the canonical source for the '582' stat used on stat callouts.

title: Workspaces
content: A Workspace is a shared planning surface for the bride, her partner, and any family member or planner she invites in. Workspaces have permissions (view / comment / edit) so the MIL can see the venue but not the budget. Each Workspace is wedding-specific.

title: Vendor Briefs
content: Vendor Briefs are pre-built one-pagers the bride sends to a vendor — photographer, decorator, caterer, MUA, choreographer, DJ. Each brief captures the muhurat, ceremony details, must-have shots / dishes / songs, and reference moodboards. Briefs cut down on the 'wait what time is the haldi' WhatsApp messages.

title: Moodboards
content: Marigold Moodboards are visual collages the bride builds for each ceremony — mandap, lehenga, sangeet stage, reception florals. Moodboards plug directly into vendor briefs so the decorator sees the same Pinterest the bride has been hoarding.

title: Budget Tracker
content: Per-ceremony budget tracker with vendor-level line items, category breakdowns (decor / food / outfits / photography / logistics), and a 'who is paying' tag — bride's family, groom's family, the couple themselves. Used to settle the 'we have a tab for both of you' joke on the BvM post template.

title: Guest List Manager
content: Guest list is split by ceremony (sangeet, mehndi, haldi, ceremony, reception) so the bride can invite different sets of people to different events. RSVP tracking, dietary tags, and travel/accommodation needs are tracked per guest.

title: South Asian brides — primary audience
content: Primary audience is South Asian brides, typically 25-34, planning multi-event desi weddings (sangeet, mehndi, haldi, ceremony, reception). Most are diaspora — US, UK, Canada — and are coordinating between two families across geographies and time zones. They are educated, digitally fluent, and frustrated by mainstream wedding planning tools that assume one venue, one ceremony, one cake.

title: Secondary audience — moms and partners
content: Secondary audience is the bride's mom (Momzilla — opinionated, status-conscious, deeply involved) and the partner (often less involved by default but invited into the Workspace). Content acknowledges these characters but always speaks TO the bride, not about her.

title: Cultural context — desi wedding specifics
content: Desi weddings have specifics no mainstream planner accounts for: muhurat (auspicious timings, often 2-5am), multiple ceremonies across 3-5 days, MIL-driven guest list expansion, baraat coordination (horse, band, route), mandap décor, lehenga drama, sangeet choreography rehearsals, and return gift logistics for hundreds of guests. Content should reference these specifics by name — never generic 'wedding'.

title: Voice — best-friend-who-just-got-married
content: Voice is the best friend who just got married and is texting you from inside the chaos. Real talk, short sentences, specific details, slightly self-deprecating. Never marketing-speak ('your big day', 'walking down the aisle'). Captions end with a question or CTA.

title: Do / Don't
content: DO: reference specific desi details (mandap, baraat, muhurat, MIL WhatsApp group); use the bride's POV; end captions with a question; use Caveat asides for punchlines. DON'T: use corporate aspirational-luxury language; punch down at families/cultures/traditions; use generic wedding phrases; over-emoji (1-2 max).

title: Caption format
content: Captions are short — 3-6 short lines, broken with line returns for breathing room. End with a question or CTA. Hashtag block separated from body by two line breaks. Hashtag mix: 2-3 brand tags (#TheMarigold, #BrideConfessions, etc.) + 4-6 broader desi wedding tags (#DesiWedding, #IndianWedding, #SangeetSeason, #MIL, #WeddingPlanning).

title: 582 — total planning tasks
content: The Marigold's master checklist contains 582 distinct planning tasks across 13 phases. Used on the canonical stat callout template. The number is real — it comes from the product checklist, not marketing.

title: 13 — planning phases
content: Wedding planning is broken into 13 sequenced phases on The Marigold. Used as a stat in feature callouts ('our 13-phase planner') and quiz result product tie-ins.

title: 5 — core ceremonies tracked
content: The product treats sangeet, mehndi, haldi, ceremony, and reception as five distinct planning tracks — each with its own vendor briefs, moodboards, guest list, and timeline. Useful for stat-callout templates that emphasize the multi-event nature of desi weddings.

title: 10,000 brides — community size
content: Tagline-friendly community size. Used on the Confessional submit CTA copy: 'your secret is safe with 10,000 brides'. Treat as a soft stat — the actual count grows over time.

title: Mainstream planners — The Knot, Zola, Joy
content: Mainstream wedding planners assume one venue, one ceremony, one cake. They have no muhurat support, no multi-ceremony tracking, no concept of MIL guest list politics or sangeet choreo coordination. The Marigold positions itself as the desi-native alternative — never mentions competitors by name in content, but the gap shows up in every culturally specific reference.
